what_it_does: This file is a docstring-only package initializer for voice/engines that documents the directory as a set of per-voice HTTP TTS services, each mirroring the same Kokoro-compatible HTTP contract and exposing a fixed port.
observations: Contains no executable code or imports; only a module-level docstring.
Defines a shared HTTP contract: POST /tts with {text, voice?, speed?} returns audio/wav, GET /voices returns voice list, GET /health returns {"ok": true}.
Mandates fail-loud behavior: any error must return HTTP 500 with JSON {"error": "<Type>: <msg>"} and never a silent empty WAV.
Lists a fixed per-engine port map from 4123 (kokoro) to 4128 (qwen3tts).
States each engine runs in its own venv/process because dependencies conflict.
References voice/engines/REQUIREMENTS.md for exact per-engine install and run commands.
Mentions heavy model imports are guarded inside a lazy _engine() so importing the wrapper module does not crash.
standouts: Identical HTTP contract across all heterogeneous TTS engines.
Explicit shared port map relied on by the runtime-backend builder and bridge routing.
Fail-loud policy explicitly forbids silent empty WAV responses.
Lazy import guard described to prevent import-time dependency failures.
conventions: Uses the package __init__.py as an architecture/contract document rather than an export surface.
Follows a contract-first design by documenting endpoints, payloads, and error behavior before implementation.
Breaks the common convention that __init__.py exposes package symbols; symbols[] is empty.
concerns: The contract is only prose in a docstring; there is no runtime validation or enforcement here.
Port numbers are hardcoded in documentation rather than as programmatic data.
The lazy _engine() guard is described but not visible or verified in this file.
Dependence on an external REQUIREMENTS.md is not validated or linked programmatically.
notes: A docstring-only package contract that tells the runtime how every trial voice engine should speak HTTP and which port it lives on.
questions: How does the bridge/runtime-backend consume this port map—by parsing the docstring or from another source?
Where is the actual _engine() lazy loader implemented and how is it enforced per engine?
What is the precise content of voice/tts_service.py that these engines mirror?
How are engine processes spawned, supervised, and restarted?
Is there any code that guarantees the fail-loud 500 JSON contract is honored?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is to document the shared engines contract and port map; actual content is a docstring-only contract, so it informs but does not enforce behavior.
apparent_themes: Process and venv isolation for conflicting dependencies
intention_signals: It is trying to make every trial TTS backend look like the same Kokoro HTTP service so the bridge can route by engine name/port without per-engine logic, while keeping incompatible dependencies apart.
novelty: Using __init__.py purely as a canonical contract and routing table for a fleet of isolated microservice-style voice engines, rather than as a code-exporting package initializer.
contribution: It contributes the documented shared HTTP contract, the per-engine port map, and the operational conventions that let heterogeneous TTS engines be integrated and routed uniformly by the bridge/runtime.
summary_for_embedding: voice/engines/__init__.py is a docstring-only package initializer that defines a shared HTTP TTS service contract, a per-engine port map, and operational conventions for isolated per-voice TTS engine processes.
intention_for_embedding: It was intended to make every trial voice engine expose the same Kokoro-compatible HTTP interface so the bridge can route requests by engine name without per-engine special handling.
